agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers, designed around five bounded contexts with event-driven integration. The architecture prioritizes independent deployability of each context, real-time order tracking, and clear ownership of invariants. Core domains (Order Management, Delivery Dispatch) receive the heaviest investment; supporting domains (Restaurant, Customer, Payment) use simpler patterns. The system uses asynchronous domain events for cross-context communication, with synchronous calls only where strong consistency is required (e.g., payment capture before order confirmation).","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Customer Gateway","responsibility":"Customer-facing API: browsing restaurants/menus, placing orders, tracking deliveries, managing profiles and addresses. BFF layer that aggregates data from multiple downstream contexts.","boundaries":"Owns no domain state directly. Composes views from Order, Restaurant, and Delivery contexts. Handles authentication for customer actors."},{"name":"Restaurant Context Service","responsibility":"Restaurant onboarding, menu management, availability/hours, order acceptance/rejection, prep-time estimation. Restaurants manage their own catalog and receive inbound orders.","boundaries":"Owns Restaurant aggregate (profile, hours, zones) and Menu aggregate (items, categories, modifiers, pricing). Does NOT own order lifecycle—emits MenuItemValidated events consumed by Order context."},{"name":"Order Context Service","responsibility":"Core order lifecycle: cart assembly, order placement, validation, state machine (placed → confirmed → preparing → ready → picked-up → delivered → completed/cancelled). Protects the central business invariant: an order's total must match validated menu prices at time of placement.","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate (line items, totals, status, timestamps) and Cart aggregate (ephemeral, pre-order). Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, OrderCompleted, OrderCancelled events."},{"name":"Delivery Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Driver matching, route optimization, real-time location tracking, ETA calculation, delivery state machine (assigned → en-route-to-restaurant → at-restaurant → en-route-to-customer → delivered). This is where the competitive advantage lives—smart dispatch = faster deliveries = happier customers.","boundaries":"Owns Delivery aggregate (driver assignment, route, location trail, ETAs) and DriverAvailability aggregate (online/offline, current location, capacity). Subscribes to OrderReady; publishes DeliveryAssigned, DriverArrivedAtRestaurant, DeliveryCompleted."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment method management, authorization, capture, refunds, restaurant/driver payouts. Integrates with external payment processor (Stripe/Adyen).","boundaries":"Owns PaymentTransaction aggregate and PayoutLedger. Synchronous call from Order context for authorization at placement; capture on delivery completion via event. Anti-corruption layer wraps external payment gateway."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Cross-cutting: push notifications, SMS, email to all actor types. Stateless event consumer—reacts to domain events and dispatches notifications via appropriate channel.","boundaries":"Owns no core domain state. Owns NotificationPreference and DeliveryChannel configuration. Pure consumer of events from Order, Delivery, and Restaurant contexts."},{"name":"Driver Gateway","responsibility":"Driver-facing API: go online/offline, accept/reject delivery assignments, navigate to pickup/dropoff, confirm pickup/delivery, view earnings.","boundaries":"BFF for driver actors. Routes commands to Delivery Dispatch and reads from Delivery/Payment contexts."},{"name":"Restaurant Dashboard Gateway","responsibility":"Restaurant-facing API/UI: manage menu, accept/reject orders, update prep times, view order history and payouts.","boundaries":"BFF for restaurant actors. Routes commands to Restaurant and Order contexts."},{"name":"Event Bus","responsibility":"Asynchronous message broker for domain events. Guarantees at-least-once delivery. Enables temporal decoupling between contexts.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. No domain logic. Contexts publish and subscribe via topic-based routing."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Order Context Service","contract":"POST /orders (place order), GET /orders/{id} (status), POST /orders/{id}/cancel","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Restaurant Context Service","contract":"GET /restaurants (search/filter), GET /restaurants/{id}/menu","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Delivery Dispatch Service","contract":"GET /deliveries/{orderId}/tracking (SSE/WebSocket for real-time location + ETA)","coupling_type":"synchronous for initial connect, then streaming"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments/authorize (synchronous at order placement), POST /payments/capture (synchronous at delivery completion)","coupling_type":"synchronous, strong consistency required"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Restaurant Context Service","contract":"GET /menus/{itemId}/validate (price + availability check at order placement)","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, OrderCancelled, OrderCompleted","coupling_type":"asynchronous, fire-and-forget"},{"from":"Delivery Dispatch Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: DeliveryAssigned, DriverLocationUpdated, DeliveryCompleted. Subscribes: OrderReady","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Restaurant Context Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: OrderAccepted, OrderRejected, MenuUpdated. Subscribes: OrderPlaced","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued. Subscribes: OrderCancelled (for refund trigger)","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Notification Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Subscribes: OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, DeliveryAssigned, DriverLocationUpdated, DeliveryCompleted, OrderCancelled","coupling_type":"asynchronous, pure consumer"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"External Payment Gateway","contract":"Stripe/Adyen API for auth, capture, refund","coupling_type":"ACL-wrapped synchronous dependency on external system"}],"data_flow":"1) Customer browses restaurants/menus via Customer Gateway → Restaurant Context. 2) Customer places order → Order Context validates items against Restaurant Context, authorizes payment via Payment Service, then persists Order and publishes OrderPlaced. 3) Restaurant subscribes to OrderPlaced, accepts/rejects → publishes OrderAccepted/OrderRejected. 4) Order Context transitions to 'confirmed' or 'cancelled' accordingly. 5) Restaurant marks order ready → Order Context publishes OrderReady. 6) Delivery Dispatch subscribes to OrderReady, runs matching algorithm, assigns driver → publishes DeliveryAssigned. 7) Driver picks up, drives to customer—DriverLocationUpdated events stream every few seconds. 8) Driver confirms delivery → DeliveryCompleted event → Order Context marks completed → Payment Service captures payment. 9) Notification Service reacts to key events throughout the flow, pushing updates to all actors.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Decouples bounded contexts temporally. Restaurant doesn't need to know about Delivery; Delivery doesn't need to know about Payment. Each context reacts to events it cares about. Enables independent scaling and deployment."},{"pattern":"Bounded Context per Service","rationale":"Each context owns its data, its model, and its language. 'Order' means something different in Restaurant context (an incoming request) vs. Delivery context (a pickup task). Separate services prevent model corruption."},{"pattern":"Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Three distinct actor types (customer, driver, restaurant) with very different UI needs. BFF gateways compose views and tailor APIs to each frontend, avoiding a bloated monolithic API."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer","rationale":"Payment Service wraps external gateway (Stripe/Adyen) in an ACL. Prevents external model leaking into domain. Makes payment provider swappable without touching core domain."},{"pattern":"Saga / Process Manager","rationale":"Order placement spans multiple contexts (validate menu, authorize payment, notify restaurant). A saga in the Order Context orchestrates this multi-step process with compensating actions on failure (e.g., release payment auth if restaurant rejects)."},{"pattern":"CQRS (lightweight)","rationale":"Delivery tracking is read-heavy with high-frequency location updates. Separate the write model (driver reports location) from the read model (customer streams location). Write to an append-only location log; project to a materialized view for fast reads."},{"pattern":"State Machine","rationale":"Both Order and Delivery have well-defined lifecycle states with strict transition rules. State machines make invalid transitions impossible and make the system auditable."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, contains LineItems, tracks status via state machine, holds price snapshot)","Cart (ephemeral aggregate, pre-order assembly, no persistence guarantee)"]},{"name":"Delivery Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, links orderId + driverId, tracks delivery state machine, holds route and ETAs)","DriverAvailability (root: driverId, online/offline status, current location, active delivery count)","LocationTrail (append-only, driverId + timestamped coordinates)"]},{"name":"Restaurant","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Restaurant (root: restaurantId, profile, hours, delivery zones, active/inactive status)","Menu (root: menuId, belongs to restaurant, contains MenuItems with modifiers and pricing)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: transactionId, links orderId, tracks auth/capture/refund lifecycle)","PayoutLedger (root: ledgerId, tracks restaurant and driver earnings, settlement periods)"]},{"name":"Customer","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CustomerProfile (root: customerId, name, contact, addresses, saved payment methods reference)","OrderHistory (read model, projected from Order events)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Restaurant","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Restaurant owns menu truth. Order validates against Restaurant's published menu data at placement time. Restaurant dictates item schema; Order conforms."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Delivery Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order publishes OrderReady; Delivery reacts. Order dictates when a delivery should begin. Delivery owns all dispatch logic independently."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order requests authorization and capture. Payment conforms to Order's lifecycle events for timing."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service / Published Language. Order publishes well-defined events; Notification subscribes without influencing Order's model."},{"upstream":"Delivery Dispatch","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service / Published Language. Same pattern—Delivery publishes tracking events; Notification consumes."},{"upstream":"Payment","downstream":"External Payment Gateway","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer. Payment wraps Stripe/Adyen. External model is translated at the boundary."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Event-driven integration between contexts instead of synchronous REST everywhere","gains":"Loose coupling, independent deployability, resilience (contexts operate during partial outages), natural audit log","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency (customer might see stale status briefly), operational complexity of managing a message broker, harder to debug distributed flows","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST between all services—simpler to reason about but creates tight coupling and cascading failure risk","Shared database—zero latency but destroys context boundaries and creates deployment coupling"]},{"decision":"Synchronous payment authorization at order placement","gains":"Strong consistency where it matters most—never confirm an order without payment guarantee. Avoids the nightmare of confirmed-but-unpayable orders.","sacrifices":"Order placement latency increases by payment gateway round-trip (~200-500ms). Single point of failure if payment service is down.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment with compensating cancellation—faster placement but risk of confirming orders that can't be paid, creating bad UX when we cancel retroactively"]},{"decision":"Separate BFF gateways per actor type","gains":"APIs tailored to each frontend's needs. Independent deployment of customer, driver, and restaurant experiences. Different scaling profiles (customer reads >> driver writes).","sacrifices":"More services to maintain. Some view composition logic duplicated. Need to keep BFFs thin to avoid logic creep.","alternatives_considered":["Single unified API gateway—fewer services but becomes a monolithic bottleneck and couples all actor types' release cycles"]},{"decision":"CQRS for delivery tracking","gains":"Write-optimized location ingestion (high-frequency driver pings) separated from read-optimized customer tracking view. Can scale reads independently during peak dinner rush.","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure (read store, projection logic). Small lag between driver location update and customer seeing it (acceptable—sub-second with streaming projection).","alternatives_considered":["Single model for both read and write—simpler but the write frequency (every 3-5s per active driver) would make read queries expensive at scale"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Provider","evolution_path":"ACL around payment gateway means swapping from Stripe to Adyen (or adding a second provider for redundancy/cost optimization) requires changes only inside Payment Service. Zero impact on Order or other contexts."},{"area":"Dispatch Algorithm","evolution_path":"Matching algorithm is encapsulated inside Delivery Dispatch. Can evolve from simple nearest-driver to ML-based optimization without touching any other context. Input (OrderReady event + driver locations) and output (DeliveryAssigned event) remain stable."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Notification Service is a pure event consumer. Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app chat) or new event triggers requires no changes to upstream contexts."},{"area":"Restaurant Onboarding","evolution_path":"Restaurant context can evolve independently—add features like dynamic pricing, AI-generated menu descriptions, multi-location support without affecting Order or Delivery."},{"area":"New Actor Types","evolution_path":"Adding new actors (e.g., corporate accounts, grocery stores) means adding new BFF gateways and potentially new bounded contexts without restructuring existing ones."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order State Machine","reason":"The order lifecycle (placed → confirmed → preparing → ready → picked-up → delivered) is the backbone of the platform. Changes here cascade to every context that subscribes to order events. This should be rigid and only change deliberately."},{"area":"Domain Event Schemas","reason":"Published events are contracts between contexts. Changing event shapes requires coordinated migration across all consumers. Use versioned schemas and additive-only changes."},{"area":"Payment Authorization Flow","reason":"The synchronous auth-at-placement, capture-at-delivery flow is a business-critical invariant. Changing this pattern (e.g., to post-delivery billing) has regulatory, fraud, and UX implications."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Distributed saga failure modes during order placement (menu validation → payment auth → restaurant notification). Partial failures can leave orders in inconsistent states across contexts.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement explicit saga orchestrator in Order Context with compensating actions: if payment auth fails → cancel order; if restaurant rejects → release payment auth and refund. Add dead-letter queues and alerting for stuck sagas. Ensure idempotency on all saga steps."},{"risk":"Driver location tracking at scale: thousands of drivers pinging every 3-5 seconds creates high write throughput and real-time streaming load.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use CQRS with append-only location store (e.g., TimescaleDB or Redis Streams). Project to materialized read view. Use WebSocket/SSE multiplexing at gateway layer. Consider geospatial indexing (PostGIS, Redis GEO) for proximity queries in dispatch."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication: at-least-once delivery means consumers must handle duplicates and potentially out-of-order events.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make all event handlers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers within each aggregate. Consumers track last-processed sequence to detect duplicates. Use partitioned topics keyed by orderId/deliveryId to maintain per-entity ordering."},{"risk":"Peak hour thundering herd: dinner rush creates correlated load spikes across all services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement backpressure at BFF gateways. Use queue-based load leveling for non-critical paths (notifications). Auto-scale Delivery Dispatch and Order Service independently. Pre-warm caches for restaurant menus."},{"risk":"Menu price mismatch: customer sees one price, but restaurant updates menu between browsing and ordering.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Snapshot menu item prices into the Order at placement time after re-validating against Restaurant context. If price changed, show customer the updated price for confirmation before proceeding."},{"risk":"Driver fraud / GPS spoofing: drivers reporting false locations to game the dispatch algorithm.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Server-side anomaly detection on location trails (impossible speed, teleportation). Cross-reference with expected route. Flag anomalies for review rather than auto-blocking to avoid false positives."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with a modular monolith organized by bounded context, using in-process event bus. Extract to microservices only when independent scaling or deployment is needed. Order and Delivery contexts will likely be the first to extract. This avoids premature distributed systems complexity while preserving clean boundaries. Use a shared PostgreSQL instance with separate schemas per context (schema-level isolation) initially.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/order/domain/Order.ts — Order aggregate with state machine","src/order/domain/events/ — OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, etc.","src/order/application/PlaceOrderSaga.ts — Orchestrates validation, payment, confirmation","src/delivery/domain/Delivery.ts — Delivery aggregate with state machine","src/delivery/domain/DriverAvailability.ts — Driver online/offline, location","src/delivery/application/DispatchService.ts — Matching algorithm","src/restaurant/domain/Restaurant.ts — Restaurant aggregate","src/restaurant/domain/Menu.ts — Menu aggregate with items and pricing","src/payment/domain/PaymentTransaction.ts — Payment lifecycle","src/payment/infrastructure/StripeACL.ts — Anti-corruption layer for Stripe","src/notification/application/NotificationHandler.ts — Event consumer","src/shared/events/EventBus.ts — In-process event bus (extract to broker later)","src/shared/events/DomainEvent.ts — Base event type with versioning","src/gateway/customer/ — Customer BFF routes","src/gateway/driver/ — Driver BFF routes","src/gateway/restaurant/ — Restaurant BFF routes"],"patterns_to_follow":"Aggregate roots enforce invariants. All state changes go through aggregate methods that emit domain events. Repositories abstract persistence. Use factory methods for complex aggregate creation. State machines use explicit transition tables (not if/else chains). Events are immutable value objects with schema version. Sagas use explicit step tracking with compensating actions.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Don't share database tables across bounded contexts—even in the modular monolith, use separate schemas","Don't put business logic in BFF gateways—they compose and delegate only","Don't use synchronous cross-context calls for non-critical paths (notifications, analytics)—always use events","Don't model the delivery as part of the Order aggregate—they have different lifecycles and different consistency boundaries","Don't build a custom real-time system from scratch—use proven tools (Redis Streams, Socket.io, or a managed service) for driver location streaming","Don't skip the saga/process manager—ad-hoc distributed coordination via hope-driven development will fail at scale","Don't over-engineer the dispatch algorithm initially—start with nearest-available-driver and iterate based on real data"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Bounded context boundaries and aggregate design—these align well with the natural seams of food delivery","Event-driven integration pattern—proven at scale by Uber Eats, DoorDash, Deliveroo","Order and Delivery state machines—well-understood domain with clear lifecycle states","Payment ACL pattern—standard approach for external payment integration"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact dispatch algorithm design—requires domain expertise on driver behavior, geography, and optimization constraints","Scaling thresholds for when to extract from modular monolith to microservices—depends on actual traffic patterns","Real-time tracking infrastructure specifics—multiple valid options (Redis Streams vs. Kafka vs. managed services) and the choice depends on ops team capability and cloud provider"],"assumptions":["Platform operates in a single geographic region initially (multi-region adds significant complexity to dispatch and data residency)","Order volume is in the thousands-per-hour range initially, not millions (affects infrastructure choices)","Three actor types: customer, restaurant, driver. No B2B/corporate ordering initially.","Single-restaurant orders only (no multi-restaurant cart). Multi-restaurant would significantly complicate the Order aggregate and delivery logistics.","Payment is pre-authorized at order time (not cash-on-delivery, which would change the payment flow significantly)"]}}

Core domains (Order Management, Delivery Dispatch) receive the heaviest investment; supporting domains (Restaurant, Customer, Payment) use simpler patterns. Avoids the nightmare of confirmed-but-unpayable orders.","sacrifices":"Order placement latency increases by payment gateway round-trip (~200-500ms). Single point of failure if payment service is down.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment with compensating cancellation—faster placement but risk of confirming orders that can't be paid, creating bad UX when we cancel retroactively"]},{"decision":"Separate BFF gateways per actor type","gains":"APIs tailored to each frontend's needs. Independent deployment of customer, driver, and restaurant experiences. Different scaling profiles (customer reads >> driver writes).","sacrifices":"More services to maintain. Some view composition logic duplicated. Need to keep BFFs thin to avoid logic creep.","alternatives_considered":["Single unified API gateway—fewer services but becomes a monolithic bottleneck and couples all actor types' release cycles"]},{"decision":"CQRS for delivery tracking","gains":"Write-optimized location ingestion (high-frequency driver pings) separated from read-optimized customer tracking view. Can scale reads independently during peak dinner rush.","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure (read store, projection logic). Small lag between driver location update and customer seeing it (acceptable—sub-second with streaming projection).","alternatives_considered":["Single model for both read and write—simpler but the write frequency (every 3-5s per active driver) would make read queries expensive at scale"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Provider","evolution_path":"ACL around payment gateway means swapping from Stripe to Adyen (or adding a second provider for redundancy/cost optimization) requires changes only inside Payment Service. Zero impact on Order or other contexts."},{"area":"Dispatch Algorithm","evolution_path":"Matching algorithm is encapsulated inside Delivery Dispatch. Can evolve from simple nearest-driver to ML-based optimization without touching any other context. Input (OrderReady event + driver locations) and output (DeliveryAssigned event) remain stable."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Notification Service is a pure event consumer. Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app chat) or new event triggers requires no changes to upstream contexts."},{"area":"Restaurant Onboarding","evolution_path":"Restaurant context can evolve independently—add features like dynamic pricing, AI-generated menu descriptions, multi-location support without affecting Order or Delivery."},{"area":"New Actor Types","evolution_path":"Adding new actors (e.g., corporate accounts, grocery stores) means adding new BFF gateways and potentially new bounded contexts without restructuring existing ones."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order State Machine","reason":"The order lifecycle (placed → confirmed → preparing → ready → picked-up → delivered) is the backbone of the platform. Changes here cascade to every context that subscribes to order events. This should be rigid and only change deliberately."},{"area":"Domain Event Schemas","reason":"Published events are contracts between contexts. Changing event shapes requires coordinated migration across all consumers. Use versioned schemas and additive-only changes."},{"area":"Payment Authorization Flow","reason":"The synchronous auth-at-placement, capture-at-delivery flow is a business-critical invariant. Changing this pattern (e.g., to post-delivery billing) has regulatory, fraud, and UX implications."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Distributed saga failure modes during order placement (menu validation → payment auth → restaurant notification). Partial failures can leave orders in inconsistent states across contexts.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement explicit saga orchestrator in Order Context with compensating actions: if payment auth fails → cancel order; if restaurant rejects → release payment auth and refund. Add dead-letter queues and alerting for stuck sagas. Ensure idempotency on all saga steps."},{"risk":"Driver location tracking at scale: thousands of drivers pinging every 3-5 seconds creates high write throughput and real-time streaming load.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use CQRS with append-only location store (e.g., TimescaleDB or Redis Streams). Project to materialized read view. Use WebSocket/SSE multiplexing at gateway layer. Consider geospatial indexing (PostGIS, Redis GEO) for proximity queries in dispatch."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication: at-least-once delivery means consumers must handle duplicates and potentially out-of-order events.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make all event handlers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers within each aggregate. Consumers track last-processed sequence to detect duplicates. Use partitioned topics keyed by orderId/deliveryId to maintain per-entity ordering."},{"risk":"Peak hour thundering herd: dinner rush creates correlated load spikes across all services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement backpressure at BFF gateways. Use queue-based load leveling for non-critical paths (notifications). Auto-scale Delivery Dispatch and Order Service independently. Pre-warm caches for restaurant menus."},{"risk":"Menu price mismatch: customer sees one price, but restaurant updates menu between browsing and ordering.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Snapshot menu item prices into the Order at placement time after re-validating against Restaurant context. If price changed, show customer the updated price for confirmation before proceeding."},{"risk":"Driver fraud / GPS spoofing: drivers reporting false locations to game the dispatch algorithm.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Server-side anomaly detection on location trails (impossible speed, teleportation). Cross-reference with expected route. Flag anomalies for review rather than auto-blocking to avoid false positives."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with a modular monolith organized by bounded context, using in-process event bus. Extract to microservices only when independent scaling or deployment is needed. Order and Delivery contexts will likely be the first to extract. This avoids premature distributed systems complexity while preserving clean boundaries. Use a shared PostgreSQL instance with separate schemas per context (schema-level isolation) initially.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/order/domain/Order.ts — Order aggregate with state machine","src/order/domain/events/ — OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, etc.","src/order/application/PlaceOrderSaga.ts — Orchestrates validation, payment, confirmation","src/delivery/domain/Delivery.ts — Delivery aggregate with state machine","src/delivery/domain/DriverAvailability.ts — Driver online/offline, location","src/delivery/application/DispatchService.ts — Matching algorithm","src/restaurant/domain/Restaurant.ts — Restaurant aggregate","src/restaurant/domain/Menu.ts — Menu aggregate with items and pricing","src/payment/domain/PaymentTransaction.ts — Payment lifecycle","src/payment/infrastructure/StripeACL.ts — Anti-corruption layer for Stripe","src/notification/application/NotificationHandler.ts — Event consumer","src/shared/events/EventBus.ts — In-process event bus (extract to broker later)","src/shared/events/DomainEvent.ts — Base event type with versioning","src/gateway/customer/ — Customer BFF routes","src/gateway/driver/ — Driver BFF routes","src/gateway/restaurant/ — Restaurant BFF routes"],"patterns_to_follow":"Aggregate roots enforce invariants. All state changes go through aggregate methods that emit domain events. Repositories abstract persistence. Use factory methods for complex aggregate creation. State machines use explicit transition tables (not if/else chains). Events are immutable value objects with schema version. Sagas use explicit step tracking with compensating actions.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Don't share database tables across bounded contexts—even in the modular monolith, use separate schemas","Don't put business logic in BFF gateways—they compose and delegate only","Don't use synchronous cross-context calls for non-critical paths (notifications, analytics)—always use events","Don't model the delivery as part of the Order aggregate—they have different lifecycles and different consistency boundaries","Don't build a custom real-time system from scratch—use proven tools (Redis Streams, Socket.io, or a managed service) for driver location streaming","Don't skip the saga/process manager—ad-hoc distributed coordination via hope-driven development will fail at scale","Don't over-engineer the dispatch algorithm initially—start with nearest-available-driver and iterate based on real data"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Bounded context boundaries and aggregate design—these align well with the natural seams of food delivery","Event-driven integration pattern—proven at scale by Uber Eats, DoorDash, Deliveroo","Order and Delivery state machines—well-understood domain with clear lifecycle states","Payment ACL pattern—standard approach for external payment integration"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact dispatch algorithm design—requires domain expertise on driver behavior, geography, and optimization constraints","Scaling thresholds for when to extract from modular monolith to microservices—depends on actual traffic patterns","Real-time tracking infrastructure specifics—multiple valid options (Redis Streams vs. Kafka vs. managed services) and the choice depends on ops team capability and cloud provider"],"assumptions":["Platform operates in a single geographic region initially (multi-region adds significant complexity to dispatch and data residency)","Order volume is in the thousands-per-hour range initially, not millions (affects infrastructure choices)","Three actor types: customer, restaurant, driver. No B2B/corporate ordering initially.","Single-restaurant orders only (no multi-restaurant cart). Multi-restaurant would significantly complicate the Order aggregate and delivery logistics.","Payment is pre-authorized at order time (not cash-on-delivery, which would change the payment flow significantly)"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers, designed around five bounded contexts with event-driven integration. The architecture prioritizes independent deployability of each context, real-time order tracking, and clear ownership of invariants. Core domains (Order Management, Delivery Dispatch) receive the heaviest investment; supporting domains (Restaurant, Customer, Payment) use simpler patterns. The system uses asynchronous domain events for cross-context communication, with synchronous calls only where strong consistency is required (e.g., payment capture before order confirmation).","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Customer Gateway","responsibility":"Customer-facing API: browsing restaurants/menus, placing orders, tracking deliveries, managing profiles and addresses. BFF layer that aggregates data from multiple downstream contexts.","boundaries":"Owns no domain state directly. Composes views from Order, Restaurant, and Delivery contexts. Handles authentication for customer actors."},{"name":"Restaurant Context Service","responsibility":"Restaurant onboarding, menu management, availability/hours, order acceptance/rejection, prep-time estimation. Restaurants manage their own catalog and receive inbound orders.","boundaries":"Owns Restaurant aggregate (profile, hours, zones) and Menu aggregate (items, categories, modifiers, pricing). Does NOT own order lifecycle—emits MenuItemValidated events consumed by Order context."},{"name":"Order Context Service","responsibility":"Core order lifecycle: cart assembly, order placement, validation, state machine (placed → confirmed → preparing → ready → picked-up → delivered → completed/cancelled). Protects the central business invariant: an order's total must match validated menu prices at time of placement.","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate (line items, totals, status, timestamps) and Cart aggregate (ephemeral, pre-order). Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, OrderCompleted, OrderCancelled events."},{"name":"Delivery Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Driver matching, route optimization, real-time location tracking, ETA calculation, delivery state machine (assigned → en-route-to-restaurant → at-restaurant → en-route-to-customer → delivered). This is where the competitive advantage lives—smart dispatch = faster deliveries = happier customers.","boundaries":"Owns Delivery aggregate (driver assignment, route, location trail, ETAs) and DriverAvailability aggregate (online/offline, current location, capacity). Subscribes to OrderReady; publishes DeliveryAssigned, DriverArrivedAtRestaurant, DeliveryCompleted."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment method management, authorization, capture, refunds, restaurant/driver payouts. Integrates with external payment processor (Stripe/Adyen).","boundaries":"Owns PaymentTransaction aggregate and PayoutLedger. Synchronous call from Order context for authorization at placement; capture on delivery completion via event. Anti-corruption layer wraps external payment gateway."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Cross-cutting: push notifications, SMS, email to all actor types. Stateless event consumer—reacts to domain events and dispatches notifications via appropriate channel.","boundaries":"Owns no core domain state. Owns NotificationPreference and DeliveryChannel configuration. Pure consumer of events from Order, Delivery, and Restaurant contexts."},{"name":"Driver Gateway","responsibility":"Driver-facing API: go online/offline, accept/reject delivery assignments, navigate to pickup/dropoff, confirm pickup/delivery, view earnings.","boundaries":"BFF for driver actors. Routes commands to Delivery Dispatch and reads from Delivery/Payment contexts."},{"name":"Restaurant Dashboard Gateway","responsibility":"Restaurant-facing API/UI: manage menu, accept/reject orders, update prep times, view order history and payouts.","boundaries":"BFF for restaurant actors. Routes commands to Restaurant and Order contexts."},{"name":"Event Bus","responsibility":"Asynchronous message broker for domain events. Guarantees at-least-once delivery. Enables temporal decoupling between contexts.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. No domain logic. Contexts publish and subscribe via topic-based routing."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Order Context Service","contract":"POST /orders (place order), GET /orders/{id} (status), POST /orders/{id}/cancel","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Restaurant Context Service","contract":"GET /restaurants (search/filter), GET /restaurants/{id}/menu","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Delivery Dispatch Service","contract":"GET /deliveries/{orderId}/tracking (SSE/WebSocket for real-time location + ETA)","coupling_type":"synchronous for initial connect, then streaming"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments/authorize (synchronous at order placement), POST /payments/capture (synchronous at delivery completion)","coupling_type":"synchronous, strong consistency required"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Restaurant Context Service","contract":"GET /menus/{itemId}/validate (price + availability check at order placement)","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime dependency"},{"from":"Order Context Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderReady, OrderCancelled, OrderCompleted","coupling_type":"asynchronous, fire-and-forget"},{"from":"Delivery Dispatch Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: DeliveryAssigned, DriverLocationUpdated, DeliveryCompleted. Subscribes: OrderReady","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Restaurant Context Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: OrderAccepted, OrderRejected, MenuUpdated. Subscribes: OrderPlaced","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued. Subscribes: OrderCancelled (for refund trigger)","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"},{"from":"Notification Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Subscribes: OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, DeliveryAssigned, DriverLocationUpdated, DeliveryCompleted, OrderCancelled","coupling_type":"asynchronous, pure consumer"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"External Payment Gateway","contract":"Stripe/Adyen API for auth, capture, refund","coupling_type":"ACL-wrapped synchronous dependency on external system"}],"data_flow":"1) Customer browses restaurants/menus via Customer Gateway → Restaurant Context. 2) Customer places order → Order Context validates items against Restaurant Context, authorizes payment via Payment Service, then persists Order and publishes OrderPlaced. 3) Restaurant subscribes to OrderPlaced, accepts/rejects → publishes OrderAccepted/OrderRejected. 4) Order Context transitions to 'confirmed' or 'cancelled' accordingly. 5) Restaurant marks order ready → Order Context publishes OrderReady. 6) Delivery Dispatch subscribes to OrderReady, runs matching algorithm, assigns driver → publishes DeliveryAssigned. 7) Driver picks up, drives to customer—DriverLocationUpdated events stream every few seconds. 8) Driver confirms delivery → DeliveryCompleted event → Order Context marks completed → Payment Service captures payment. 9) Notification Service reacts to key events throughout the flow, pushing updates to all actors.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Decouples bounded contexts temporally. Restaurant doesn't need to know about Delivery; Delivery doesn't need to know about Payment. Each context reacts to events it cares about. Enables independent scaling and deployment."},{"pattern":"Bounded Context per Service","rationale":"Each context owns its data, its model, and its language. 'Order' means something different in Restaurant context (an incoming request) vs. Delivery context (a pickup task). Separate services prevent model corruption."},{"pattern":"Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Three distinct actor types (customer, driver, restaurant) with very different UI needs. BFF gateways compose views and tailor APIs to each frontend, avoiding a bloated monolithic API."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer","rationale":"Payment Service wraps external gateway (Stripe/Adyen) in an ACL. Prevents external model leaking into domain. Makes payment provider swappable without touching core domain."},{"pattern":"Saga / Process Manager","rationale":"Order placement spans multiple contexts (validate menu, authorize payment, notify restaurant). A saga in the Order Context orchestrates this multi-step process with compensating actions on failure (e.g., release payment auth if restaurant rejects)."},{"pattern":"CQRS (lightweight)","rationale":"Delivery tracking is read-heavy with high-frequency location updates. Separate the write model (driver reports location) from the read model (customer streams location). Write to an append-only location log; project to a materialized view for fast reads."},{"pattern":"State Machine","rationale":"Both Order and Delivery have well-defined lifecycle states with strict transition rules. State machines make invalid transitions impossible and make the system auditable."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, contains LineItems, tracks status via state machine, holds price snapshot)","Cart (ephemeral aggregate, pre-order assembly, no persistence guarantee)"]},{"name":"Delivery Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, links orderId + driverId, tracks delivery state machine, holds route and ETAs)","DriverAvailability (root: driverId, online/offline status, current location, active delivery count)","LocationTrail (append-only, driverId + timestamped coordinates)"]},{"name":"Restaurant","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Restaurant (root: restaurantId, profile, hours, delivery zones, active/inactive status)","Menu (root: menuId, belongs to restaurant, contains MenuItems with modifiers and pricing)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: transactionId, links orderId, tracks auth/capture/refund lifecycle)","PayoutLedger (root: ledgerId, tracks restaurant and driver earnings, settlement periods)"]},{"name":"Customer","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CustomerProfile (root: customerId, name, contact, addresses, saved payment methods reference)","OrderHistory (read model, projected from Order events)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Restaurant","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Restaurant owns menu truth. Order validates against Restaurant's published menu data at placement time. Restaurant dictates item schema; Order conforms."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Delivery Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order publishes OrderReady; Delivery reacts. Order dictates when a delivery should begin. Delivery owns all dispatch logic independently."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order requests authorization and capture. Payment conforms to Order's lifecycle events for timing."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service / Published Language. Order publishes well-defined events; Notification subscribes without influencing Order's model."},{"upstream":"Delivery Dispatch","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service / Published Language. Same pattern—Delivery publishes tracking events; Notification consumes."},{"upstream":"Payment","downstream":"External Payment Gateway","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer. Payment wraps Stripe/Adyen. External model is translated at the boundary."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Event-driven integration between contexts instead of synchronous REST everywhere","gains":"Loose coupling, independent deployability, resilience (contexts operate during partial outages), natural audit log","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency (customer might see stale status briefly), operational complexity of managing a message broker, harder to debug distributed flows","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST between all services—simpler to reason about but creates tight coupling and cascading failure risk","Shared database—zero latency but destroys context boundaries and creates deployment coupling"]},{"decision":"Synchronous payment authorization at order placement","gains":"Strong consistency where it matters most—never confirm an order without payment guarantee. Avoids the nightmare of confirmed-but-unpayable orders.","sacrifices":"Order placement latency increases by payment gateway round-trip (~200-500ms). Single point of failure if payment service is down.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment with compensating cancellation—faster placement but risk of confirming orders that can't be paid, creating bad UX when we cancel retroactively"]},{"decision":"Separate BFF gateways per actor type","gains":"APIs tailored to each frontend's needs. Independent deployment of customer, driver, and restaurant experiences. Different scaling profiles (customer reads >> driver writes).","sacrifices":"More services to maintain. Some view composition logic duplicated. Need to keep BFFs thin to avoid logic creep.","alternatives_considered":["Single unified API gateway—fewer services but becomes a monolithic bottleneck and couples all actor types' release cycles"]},{"decision":"CQRS for delivery tracking","gains":"Write-optimized location ingestion (high-frequency driver pings) separated from read-optimized customer tracking view. Can scale reads independently during peak dinner rush.","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure (read store, projection logic). Small lag between driver location update and customer seeing it (acceptable—sub-second with streaming projection).","alternatives_considered":["Single model for both read and write—simpler but the write frequency (every 3-5s per active driver) would make read queries expensive at scale"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Provider","evolution_path":"ACL around payment gateway means swapping from Stripe to Adyen (or adding a second provider for redundancy/cost optimization) requires changes only inside Payment Service. Zero impact on Order or other contexts."},{"area":"Dispatch Algorithm","evolution_path":"Matching algorithm is encapsulated inside Delivery Dispatch. Can evolve from simple nearest-driver to ML-based optimization without touching any other context. Input (OrderReady event + driver locations) and output (DeliveryAssigned event) remain stable."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Notification Service is a pure event consumer. Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app chat) or new event triggers requires no changes to upstream contexts."},{"area":"Restaurant Onboarding","evolution_path":"Restaurant context can evolve independently—add features like dynamic pricing, AI-generated menu descriptions, multi-location support without affecting Order or Delivery."},{"area":"New Actor Types","evolution_path":"Adding new actors (e.g., corporate accounts, grocery stores) means adding new BFF gateways and potentially new bounded contexts without restructuring existing ones."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order State Machine","reason":"The order lifecycle (placed → confirmed → preparing → ready → picked-up → delivered) is the backbone of the platform. Changes here cascade to every context that subscribes to order events. This should be rigid and only change deliberately."},{"area":"Domain Event Schemas","reason":"Published events are contracts between contexts. Changing event shapes requires coordinated migration across all consumers. Use versioned schemas and additive-only changes."},{"area":"Payment Authorization Flow","reason":"The synchronous auth-at-placement, capture-at-delivery flow is a business-critical invariant. Changing this pattern (e.g., to post-delivery billing) has regulatory, fraud, and UX implications."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Distributed saga failure modes during order placement (menu validation → payment auth → restaurant notification). Partial failures can leave orders in inconsistent states across contexts.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement explicit saga orchestrator in Order Context with compensating actions: if payment auth fails → cancel order; if restaurant rejects → release payment auth and refund. Add dead-letter queues and alerting for stuck sagas. Ensure idempotency on all saga steps."},{"risk":"Driver location tracking at scale: thousands of drivers pinging every 3-5 seconds creates high write throughput and real-time streaming load.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use CQRS with append-only location store (e.g., TimescaleDB or Redis Streams). Project to materialized read view. Use WebSocket/SSE multiplexing at gateway layer. Consider geospatial indexing (PostGIS, Redis GEO) for proximity queries in dispatch."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication: at-least-once delivery means consumers must handle duplicates and potentially out-of-order events.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make all event handlers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers within each aggregate. Consumers track last-processed sequence to detect duplicates. Use queue-based load leveling for non-critical paths (notifications). Auto-scale Delivery Dispatch and Order Service independently. Pre-warm caches for restaurant menus."},{"risk":"Menu price mismatch: customer sees one price, but restaurant updates menu between browsing and ordering.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Snapshot menu item prices into the Order at placement time after re-validating against Restaurant context.
